data.title: Global Net Lease: Investment Grade 8% Dividend Yield, Coverage Slipping
data.description:
Global Net Lease (GNL) is up 27% over the last year, in a recovery that could have legs on the back of the REIT's cheap multiple to adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO"). GNL generated AFFO of $48.5 million during its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter, around $0.22 per share. This was down from $78.3 million, or $0.34 per share, in the year-ago comp. Critically, GNL expects AFFO to dip again in 2026, with guidance for the full year placing AFFO between $0.80 per share and $0.84 per share. This would be a dip at the low end from AFFO of $0.99 for the full year 2025.

GNL last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.19 per share, kept unchanged from the prior distribution, and $0.76 per share annualized for an 8% dividend yield. GNL's dividend history is one that tells a story of back-to-back cuts and poor coverage, with the yield spiking as high as 21% at the peak of the collapse in confidence around the REIT's capacity to maintain the prior distributions.

I view the commons at their current level as more attractive but not yet a comfortable buy, with the current dividend yield providing 373 basis points of income above the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). GNL saw its credit rating upgraded to investment grade at "BBB-" by Fitch Ratings in October, with this rating affirmed in February, capping the REIT's transformation from a highly leveraged, externally managed, mixed-property REIT to an internally managed security whose back-to-back dividend cuts and capital recycling program have allowed for greater focus on single-tenant tenants and to reduce the debt held on its balance sheet. GNL's 7.25% Series A Preferreds (GNL.PR.A) and its 6.875% Series B Preferreds (GNL.PR.B) also had their credit rating upgraded to "BB."

Lower Leverage, Revenue Dip, And Dividend Coverage Risk

GNL held around $2.57 billion of total debt as of the end of the fourth quarter, with the trendline for this having dipped markedly. This was cited by Fitch as the core reason for the credit rating upgrade, with GNL's net debt to adjusted EBITDA now at 6.7x, down from 2024 when it was around 7.8x. The consistent repayment of debt has essentially been done partially on the back of the dividend cuts, with the coveted investment-grade rating a catalyst for the rally in GNL's common shares. The Series A preferreds are down around 3% year-to-date, tracking the volatility seen in fixed income since the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz caused energy prices to spike. This has raised inflation expectations and pushed up Treasury rates. The REIT held 820 properties spread across 41 million square feet and had a 97% occupancy rate as of the end of its fourth quarter.

GNL faces just $95 million in debt maturing this year, which is easily covered by cash and cash equivalents, which stood at $180.11 million as of the end of the fourth quarter. $631 million is coming up for repayment next year. GNL's debt had a weighted average debt maturity of 3 years, with the REIT having reduced its net debt by at least $2.2 billion since the fourth quarter of 2024. Total liquidity as of the end of the fourth quarter stood at $962 million after the inclusion of a revolving credit facility, which places the comfortable repayment of next year's debt maturity into view.

I last covered GNL as a "Hold" in August 2025, with the view that we had seen the last of the dividend cuts and the current quarterly distribution was safe. The REIT is currently covering its dividend by 116% through its fourth-quarter AFFO, a payout ratio of around 86.40%. While this sits below the 90% requirement for REITs, AFFO dipped by around 12 cents per share from its year-ago quarter. Critically, the midpoint of GNL's AFFO guidance for 2026 at $0.82 per share would imply that dividend coverage is set to dip again to 108%.

This places another dividend cut into view and forms a core risk for GNL's common shareholders. The Series A preferreds are a healthy choice here, with their $1.81 annual coupon driving an 8% current yield. They're trading at a 9.36% discount to their $25 per share redemption value and offer a level of income on par with the commons with less downside volatility risk in the event the common shares are cut again.

Conclusion

GNL's FFO guidance for 2026 raised the risk of another dividend cut, even with more than 100% coverage, and it's difficult to see the commons as a prudent investment against this risk. However, the fact that the REIT repurchased 17.2 million common shares through 2025 is a vote of confidence in the common equity at its current level and the possible stickiness of the dividend as the specter of slipping dividend coverage looms. I am rating GNL as a "Hold."

data.title: FLBR: From Worst In The World In 2024 To 2nd Best In 2026 And The Window Remains Open (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am raising my recommendation from hold to buy on the Franklin FTSE Brazil ETF (FLBR). My initial coverage article of this ETF was published on June 30, 2024, and my last article was published on July 11, 2025. My intention in this article is to answer the question: How did Brazil go from being the worst ETF country in 2024 to becoming the second-best ETF country in 2026?

Franklin FTSE Brazil ETF

When investors want exposure to Brazilian shares, they typically look at the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (NYSEARCA:EWZ), the ETF from the management company BlackRock. However, in this article I will address FLBR, from manager Franklin Templeton. The reason? Although FLBR has a much smaller AUM than EWZ, its expense ratio is just 0.19%, which increases the investor's return in the long term.

But what are the differences between ETFs? FLBR tracks the FTSE Brazil RIC Capped Index, while EWZ tracks the MSCI Brazil 25/50 Index. The holdings are quite similar, but EWZ has relatively greater exposure to Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU). In the last three years, FLBR has had a slight advantage over EWZ, so the choice is personal to each investor.

Context

Although the ETF covered in this article is FLBR, I will quickly use EWZ for an assertive macro comparison. At the end of 2024, EWZ, the ETF that tracks Brazilian assets, presented the worst performance in the world, and Argentina, which is a neighboring country to Brazil, presented the best performance, ironically.

At the time, investors were optimistic about President Milei's libertarian policies in Argentina: spending cuts, increased efficiency, and growth. In Brazil, the recipe was practically the opposite: increased spending, high debt, low growth, high-interest rates, and high family debt. The most curious thing is that we are in April 2026, and Brazil continues to have all the characteristics listed above, but now its ETFs have the second-best performance in the world, an increase of 30% on the year.

But returning to the central question of this article: how did Brazil go from being the worst country ETF in 2024 to becoming the second-best country ETF in 2026? You will find analysts stating that the Brazilian stock market rose for several reasons, such as attractive valuation, interest rate differential between Brazil and the United States, weakening of the dollar, appreciation of currencies in emerging countries, and high flow of investments in Brazil.

All the arguments reported above are true, but the valuation was already attractive in 2024, as were the interest rates. Why have investors only now woken up to the Brazilian market? In my opinion, there are two very clear drivers that are driving the Brazilian stock market in 2026.

First Driver

The Brazilian presidential elections take place in the second half of 2026. Current president Lula's term was marked by tax increases, high family debt, and a greater perception of inflation. However, polls already indicate that the opposition (son of former president Bolsonaro) is ahead in the electoral polls.

This driver is relevant, as the perception is that the opposition will be more likely to carry out a necessary fiscal adjustment in Brazil. In my opinion, the market is pricing in the possibility of an effect similar to President Milei's victory in Argentina. But the second driver is undoubtedly the most relevant.

Second Driver

The main driver behind the rise of the Brazilian stock market is extremely irrelevant and almost imperceptible, but once you see it, you won't be able to ignore it. At the end of 2025, I published an article called "2026, the beginning of natural selection in AI," and in that article I described the thesis that would mark the beginning of the realization that AI would disrupt many businesses.

In 2026, what we are witnessing is exactly this, and the sharp drop in software stocks portrays exactly this thesis, so investors have started to look for businesses that cannot be disrupted by AI, and Brazil is a perfect match.

Brazil has an economy that is extremely sensitive to commodities. The Brazilian firm Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) is one of the largest oil companies in the world, while Vale (NYSE:VALE) is one of the largest mining companies in the world. Brazilian agribusiness does not have a world-renowned company, but it is an extremely productive sector.

Ironically, investors thought that artificial intelligence would widen the investment gap between developed countries and emerging countries, but what happens is that emerging countries that are highly sensitive to commodities (thus not disrupted by AI) are extremely promising. But is it still worth investing?

Valuation

When you analyze the price-to-earnings ratio of Brazilian stocks over the last 10 years, the first perception is that the market is expensive and you shouldn't invest. Now let's see another perception.

In my opinion, many years ago, Brazil was not so well positioned in the current context of the global economy. It is a country with abundant energy, a producer of essential commodities such as oil, ore, and food, and has room to cut interest rates and carry out a fiscal adjustment in the medium term.

Multiple repricings are underway, and given the strong momentum, it is highly likely that Brazil will reach the multiple of emerging markets. If the multiple rises from 13.3x earnings (current) to 16.1x earnings, there is an upside of 21%.

Therefore, I am raising the recommendation from hold to buy for FLBR. I'm not recommending buying now that the price has gone up! It's worth remembering that at the beginning of 2026 I published an article with my portfolio of recommended stocks, and in that portfolio there is a mix of American and Brazilian stocks that have easily beaten the S&P 500 in 2026. On February 5, 2026, I also raised my hold to a buy rating on EWZ.

Potential Threats to the Thesis

The margin of safety has decreased significantly as prices have risen. As we can see in the graph below, returns are usually lower when the investment is made at a higher price/earnings multiple.

In parallel, Brazil has one of the highest debt/GDP ratios between peers. If this problem is not addressed after the elections, investment in Brazilian assets will be extremely risky, and the possibility of a sharp drop in prices is high.

The electoral driver is also extremely important. The market is starting to really believe that the opposition will be able to win the elections and carry out a fiscal adjustment. However, if the current president is re-elected, it is possible that expectations will not be met, and this will also lead to a drop in prices.

The Bottom Line

My intention in this article was to answer an extremely genuine question. After all, the Brazil of 2024 has changed little in relation to the Brazil of 2026. But why in 2024 did Brazilian assets present the worst performance in the world, and in 2026 they presented the second-best performance in the world?

The reader will find several arguments, such as the increase in the flow of foreign investments, devaluation of the dollar, and interest differential, but the truth is that all these arguments are generated by two drivers: expectations regarding the elections and Brazilian immunity to AI disruption.

Based on this analysis, I am raising my recommendation to hold to buy on FLBR. Although the price has increased and the margin of safety has decreased, the risk-return relationship is still very attractive.

data.title: Plains All American: Why I'm Downgrading This 8% Yield Despite The Oil Price Spike
data.description:
I last wrote about Plains All American Pipeline (PAA)/Plains GP Holdings (PAGP) back in November of last year, and rated it a strong buy with an attractive combination of current yield and expected distribution growth for the foreseeable future. Since then, it has soared higher, generating over 29% in total returns, compared to just a 4.6% total return for the S&P 500 (SPY).

Part of the catalyst for driving it higher over that period has been the war in Iran, which has driven oil prices (USO) materially higher and also increased the value of U.S.-sourced energy, since it is considered to be much more geopolitically stable than depending on oil from geopolitically uncertain regions such as the Strait of Hormuz.

In this article, I will revisit the investment thesis in light of evolving geopolitical conditions as well as the significantly increased valuation for PAA/PAGP, and share my updated outlook on the stock moving forward.

The Strait of Hormuz Shock Nobody Expected

Since the U.S. and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury against Iran in late February, Iran has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, which has resulted in a 10.1 million bpd supply loss and has been called the largest supply disruption in history by the IEA. In a matter of weeks, WTI crude has surged from being in the $60s to $90s and even over $100 a barrel on numerous occasions. The EIA has since raised its 2026 WTI forecast to $87.41 a barrel.

Average U.S. gas prices have surged by over a third since the war began, and 171 crude tankers are currently bound for the U.S. Gulf Coast versus a typical 110 in an ordinary month, as both Asia and Europe are pivoting towards North American-sourced oil to replace what they typically get from the Middle East.

PAA's Unrivaled Position in the New Energy Order

So, how does this all impact Plains All American Pipeline/Plains GP? First of all, it is important to note that PAA is a K-1 issuing MLP (AMLP), whereas PAGP is the 1099-issuing economic equivalent. The company operates over 20,000 miles of active crude oil pipeline and has 76 million barrels of commercial storage capacity across its assets. As such, it is able to handle over 9 million barrels per day of total pipeline tariff volume, with a heavy concentration in the Permian Basin, where it can handle over 7 million barrels per day there alone.

The company is structured to be fairly commodity price volatility resistant, as it generates 80.5% of its expected adjusted EBITDA in 2026 from fee-for-service contracts, minimum volume commitments, and/or acreage dedications. Meanwhile, about 75% of its asset footprint is located in Texas, either via the Permian Basin or the South Texas/Gulf Coast region. This means that its assets will likely be substantially impacted by this increased demand for U.S. energy exports, much of which flows through the Texas Gulf Coast.

The company also recently completed an acquisition that should benefit from this trend, as it acquired Cactus 3, which was formerly known as the Epic Crude Pipeline. This is the key Permian to Corpus Christi pipeline that is now serving as a vital component of American crude oil export infrastructure and should benefit from growing demand in the coming years. Not only that, but the Permian Basin itself is at the heart of U.S. export supply when it comes to oil, and PAA is well-positioned in the Permian Basin to benefit from this, as it controls over 2.8 million barrels per day of Permian takeaway capacity, with more than 5 million dedicated acres under contract.

America's Export Boom

This infrastructure sets PAA up to be a major player in America's ongoing export boom. In fact, already in April 2026, U.S. Gulf Coast crude exports are sitting at 4.9 million barrels per day, which is a record and a 23% increase from March and a 30% increase from February, showing that the war in the Middle East is truly having a positive impact on U.S. energy exports. Meanwhile, WTI premiums for delivery to Asia in July have jumped to a whopping $30 to $40 per barrel above the benchmark as Asia and Europe compete for U.S. supply. There is now potential for May exports to break the 5 million barrels per day level for the first time ever, as there are 28 supertankers already under contract versus a typical 5 at this time of the month.

What the Oil Surge Actually Means for PAA's Numbers

What does this all mean for PAA? The large majority of PAA's business model is already locked in to what it's going to generate, due to its commodity price volatility mitigation measures. PAA does have 15% commodity price exposure through its pipeline loss allowance volumes and market-based opportunities, and management itself discloses that they estimate every $10 per barrel change in WTI has an approximate $40 million impact on annualized adjusted EBITDA. This means that if WTI is now about $30 a barrel higher than when the WTI guidance was set, that leads to about $120 million in incremental EBITDA versus the original guidance, given that the guidance was $2.75 billion. That is a potential 4 to 5% EBITDA increase from pricing changes alone.

On top of that, the changing dynamic in the industry, in which North American-sourced energy is deemed to be an even more reliable source of supply, means that PAA should benefit. For starters, there will likely be higher volume production in the immediate term, which means greater demand on PAA's existing infrastructure assets, thereby making them more valuable. This could lead to additional, albeit small, profits this year alone, but also more significant growth opportunities and stronger re-contracting leverage for PAA's infrastructure network moving forward.

Thus, not only will PAA see a nice mid-single-digit percentage increase to its adjusted EBITDA and free cash flows this year, in all likelihood, but it should also see its current infrastructure increase in value and the company enjoy additional growth opportunities moving forward.

On top of that, PAA's balance sheet remains in very strong shape, with a BBB credit rating from both S&P and Fitch, and an equivalent from Moody's. The company also has well-laddered debt maturities, has plenty of liquidity, and significant free cash flow generation. Additionally, it's covering its distribution nicely to the tune of 1.5 to 1.6 times. The tailwinds to cash flows should only further strengthen its growth runway for its distribution moving forward.

The Balance Sheet Still Impresses, But the Price Tag Doesn't

That being said, value is only one side of the equation, as the other side is the valuation you pay to get that value, and the recent increase in its unit price has taken a bite out of its yield and its valuation. PAA now trades at 11.3 times EBITDA, which is well above its historic average of around 10 times and even more elevated compared to its five-year average of 9.18 times.

Sure, the assets are worth more today given the changing situation in the world, but are they really worth that much more, especially given that there is still a substantial part of PAA's business located outside of the most export-sensitive markets, and 85% of PAA's business is fee-based?

Why the Yield + Growth Math No Longer Excites

The next twelve-month distribution yield for PAA is still attractive at 8%, and for PAGP it is slightly less, though still attractive at around 7.3%. When it comes to growth, however, the outlook is less than impressive, at just a mid-single-digit distribution per unit growth CAGR. Moreover, analysts expect that EBITDA will only grow at a 1.9% CAGR through 2030, and distributed cash flow per unit is only expected to grow at a 3.3% CAGR over that period.

Thus, the yield plus growth rate outlook is not nearly as exciting as the market's price movement for PAA and PAGP would seem to indicate. As a result, the yield plus growth outlook barely exceeds 10%, perhaps settling at around 11% over time, and the elevated valuation multiple means that any meaningful multiple expansion from here is unlikely.

My Downgrade, And Why I'm Still Holding

Therefore, I'm downgrading PAA and PAGP to a hold purely on valuation, even though I do think that the underlying intrinsic value of the business has increased materially. In particular, the long-term risk for these assets has decreased, as Texas-based oil export infrastructure is more valuable than ever in a world in which the Strait of Hormuz is considered to be highly risky.

Given that the yield is still attractive, I have a large gain in my position, and I do see continued growth that should keep up with or even slightly exceed inflation for the foreseeable future. I continue to hold a moderately sized position in the company, but favor other midstream opportunities for new capital deployment in my 7–8% yielding, total return outperforming and below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DGRO: 11-Year Dividend Growth Streak Backed By Strong Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) on October 10, 2025, when I rated it a solid "hold" due to "its balanced mix of value, earnings growth, dividend growth, quality, and cost efficiency." I also found it likely to continue its impressive dividend growth streak, which is now up to 11, per Seeking Alpha.

If you're a manager or Index provider, it's fairly easy to manufacture dividend growth. All you need to do is routinely sell low-yielding stocks and replace them with higher-yielding ones, but if those replacements aren't on good financial footing, you might end up in a situation where you achieve your dividend growth objectives mainly by sacrificing total returns. Fortunately, DGRO is not one of those ETFs. Its components are delivering enough earnings growth to support future dividend growth, their payout ratios average just 42%, and in aggregate, they have increased dividends for over 22 years. DGRO has other strong fundamental characteristics that make it attractive to risk-averse investors, so for those reasons, I'm confident reiterating my solid "hold" rating on the fund. I hope you enjoy the read.

DGRO Overview

DGRO has $39 billion in assets under management, a 0.08% expense ratio, a 2.03% trailing dividend yield, and tracks the Morningstar US Dividend Growth Index, which aims to "provide exposure to securities in the Morningstar US Markets Index with a history of uninterrupted dividend growth and the capacity to sustain that growth." This description is spot on and confirms that the Index seeks companies with both a willingness and ability to increase their dividends. For the "ability" portion, the way they do that is by screening out potential yield traps (i.e., those with dividend yields in the top 10%) and insisting on positive consensus earnings forecasts and payout ratios below 75%. For the "willingness" portion, companies must be on a 5+ year dividend growth streak. That requirement might seem a little short, but the benefit is that it allows more high-growth tech stocks into the mix. Since earnings growth funds dividend growth, it helps the Index achieve its primary dividend growth objective.

The Index reconstitutes only once per year after the third Friday in December, so there's very little opportunity to manufacture dividend growth in the way I described earlier. However, DGRO's turnover rates (25-65% over the last five fiscal years) are higher compared to others like the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), which requires 10+ years of consecutive dividend increases. This makes sense though - since the five-year minimum is a lower bar to clear, more stocks can qualify.

Lastly, DGRO's components are dividend-dollar-weighted, which is essentially a blend between a cap-weighting scheme and a yield-weighting scheme. Weights are determined by the product of a stock's shares outstanding and indicated dividend yield, subject to a 3% cap, and the result of this is that several low-yielding stocks can receive high weights simply because they're so massive. That's how Apple Inc. (AAPL), a stock worth $3.8 trillion but yields just 0.40%, makes its way into DGRO's top ten list.

DGRO Performance Analysis

Since July 2014, one month following its inception, DGRO has delivered a 280.97% total return (including dividends), which works out to an 11.97% annualized return through April 15, 2026. That's slightly better than VIG's 11.48% result, and although it's behind SPY's 13.26% annualized gain, the chart below shows that SPY only really began to break away over the last couple of years. Low and non-dividend-payers like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) fueled these excess returns for SPY, and since it's a cap-weighted ETF, high concentration actually worked in its favor.

One downside for DGRO is that it didn't really offer investors much downside protection during the Q1 2020 COVID-19 crash. Over those three months, it declined by 21.92%, which wasn't much better than SPY's, and even though its "worst 12 months" figure was about 9% more, its risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were still lower. Therefore, I do think there is a slight total returns sacrifice investors must accept by prioritizing dividend growth, especially if you're bullish on the U.S. stock market over the long run.

Still, that sacrifice might be worth it. Per Portfolio Visualizer, a $10,000 investment in January 2017 with reinvested dividends would result in total portfolio income of $612 for the calendar year 2025. Interestingly, if one chose not to reinvest dividends, income would only be $502, so I think reinvestment is an important aspect. Therefore, it's probably best for younger investors looking to develop a steady stream of income later in life, while older investors with more immediate income needs might consider higher-yielding funds like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Per Seeking Alpha, it's on a 14-year dividend growth streak, which is why it's often considered a benchmark ETF to own for DGI investors.

DGRO Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for DGRO's top 20 holdings, which comprise 42.25% of the portfolio. This "under-the-hood" analysis helps us understand why DGRO is well-positioned to continue its 11-year dividend growth streak, and at the same time, allows us to compare it with VIG, SCHD, and SPY on an apples-to-apples basis. Please have a quick scan of the results, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

The first thing to highlight is DGRO's 2.12% Index Yield, which is the weighted average dividend yield of its current components. After deducting its 0.08% expense ratio, shareholders should yield 2.04%, which actually lines up quite well with the fund's 2.03% trailing yield. Estimated yields for the other three ETFs are as follows:

  • VIG: 1.72% - 0.04% = 1.68%
  • SCHD: 3.54% - 0.06% = 3.48%
  • SPY: 1.19% - 0.08% = 1.11%

In short, VIG offers a better yield than VIG and SPY but is still miles away from SCHD, and this gap in starting yield can make a big difference if your time horizon is relatively short (e.g., if you're nearing retirement). Executing a successful dividend growth strategy requires time, and in the short term, it doesn't matter how strong DGRO's dividend growth rates are - you'll likely never get the same yield on cost as SCHD. As a simple example, the rule of 70 suggests you'll need a growth rate of about 7% to double your DGRO's yield on cost to 4.08% in 10 years. To get to the same 4.08% yield, SCHD only needs 1.6% annualized dividend growth over those 10 years, and based on its historical 3-, 5-, and 10-year growth rates, that shouldn't be a problem.

However, to reiterate an earlier point, how each ETF achieves its dividend growth matters, and in my opinion, DGRO's method is much healthier. To illustrate, its components' three-year historical and one-year estimated EPS growth rates of 6.33% and 11.21% support the high-single-digit dividend growth investors need without having to "manufacture" dividend growth by routinely rotating out of low-yielding stocks and replacing them with higher-yielding ones. DGRO's 41.87% portfolio-level payout ratio is also healthy, while VIG and SPY are in an even better position to support increased payments.

In contrast, SCHD's fundamentals are more questionable. For a moment, I ask that you imagine yourself building your own dividend growth portfolio using individual stocks. When screening, would you be comfortable picking a stock whose earnings per share have declined over the last three years? If not, then SCHD's -2.53% 3Y EPS CAGR should be a red flag. Yes, its components have still increased dividends by an annualized 7.02% over that period, but that dividend growth obviously isn't supported by earnings, and that's why its 58.85% payout ratio is so high. Not coincidentally, this earnings growth problem only started showing up in 2023, right around the same time SCHD began to underperform most of its peers.

GARP and Quality Considerations

Another angle I like to consider is which ETF offers the best growth potential for its price. To do so, I've calculated the following forward PEG ratios (price-earnings-growth) for each ETF using their portfolio-level P/E ratios and 1Y estimated EPS growth rates:

  • DGRO: 18.35x / 11.21% = 1.64x
  • VIG: 20.73x / 14.17% = 1.46x
  • SCHD: 15.18x / 6.54% = 2.32x
  • SPY: 21.42x / 18.46% = 1.16x

As shown below, DGRO's 1.64x result is third-best behind SPY and VIG but still much better than SCHD's 2.32x figure. Since I'm a big believer that a dividend growth ETF should have good earnings growth potential for its price, I give the edge to VIG at the moment, but that doesn't mean DGRO is a bad choice. The ratios are close, DGRO offers a higher starting yield, and overall, it's still in pretty good shape.

That said, I do believe DGRO's quality features are the weakest of the four. My ETF Rankings System has assigned it a quality score of only 6.41/10 compared to 7.46/10, 6.88/10, and 8.57/10 for VIG, SCHD, and SPY, respectively. Key contributors include relatively low free cash flow margins of 10.44% and lower returns on equity (25.98%) and total capital (14.21%), as shown below.

Lastly, I'd like to caution against owning both DGRO and VIG. As of today, they overlap by 70%, and I can see overlap getting pushed to 75-80% over the next five years. If this materializes, holding both would be a relatively inefficient use of capital, and to give you an idea of what stocks in DGRO might soon qualify in VIG once they hit the 10+ year dividend growth mark, I've compiled the following list of possible additions for the 2027 and 2028 calendar years.
